text: All right, so presenting the case of a robotic right colectomy here. This is Adam Rothermel coming to you from the little city, big town of Zanesville, Ohio. We've got a seventy-two-year-old lady with a large tubulobilous adenoma of the cecum presented with some anemia found by one of our excellent gastroenterologists and referred to our practice for resection.

The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection, identifying the ureter visible on the lower left and the duodenum. The proximal transverse mesocolon is being peeled away, revealing the right branch of the middle colic vessel. The mesentery of the proximal transverse colon appears short, and the right branch of the middle colic vessel is taken despite no suspicion of malignancy.
The surgeon creates a mesenteric window at the terminal ileum using the robotic platform, facilitating a stable medial to lateral approach.
The surgeon takes down the hepatic flexure by dissecting through the gastrocolic ligament, sweeping the duodenum down and laterally to meet the medial-to-lateral dissection approach.

The surgeon finalizes the dissection of the hepatic flexure, leaving the lateral attachments along the white line intact until the end. A blue load robotic stapler, SureForm, is used to secure the tissue. The mesentery still requires completion.
The surgeon proceeds to take down the lateral attachments of the colon, completing the mobilization after the medial to lateral approach.

The surgeon uses a blue load stapler to transect the terminal ileum. Indocyanine green (ICG) is utilized to assess perfusion of the proximal transverse colon. The specimen is freed and positioned above the liver to clear the surgical field for the upcoming anastomosis.
The surgeon places two stay sutures using 2-0 silk to facilitate a side-to-side isoperistaltic anastomosis.
The surgeon creates a colotomy and enterotomy using scissors, preparing for the anastomosis. The procedure is performed at three times the normal speed in the video.
The surgeon completes the closure of the common channel using a 3-0 V-Loc absorbable suture, securing the tissue with precise stitching.

The surgeon completes the first layer of sutures and begins running imbricating sutures along the anastomosis. After breaking the suture halfway, a new suture is placed to continue from the back part of the common channel closure.

The surgeon brings the two ends of the bowel together and ties them with a couple of knots. The previously placed 2-0 silk stay suture is used to suspend the bowel against the abdominal wall, providing stabilization for the anastomosis. Additional reinforcement sutures are placed using the same suture.
The surgeon places the specimen into the pelvis after removal through a four-centimeter Pfannenstiel incision. The omentum is draped over the completed side-to-side isoperistaltic anastomosis.
